signature_verification._patch1_resolution: v7.5.9 PATCH 1: injected_at_epoch removed from SPHINCS+ signed message inputs. Root cause: injected_at_epoch is written by the relayer at injection time — AFTER the oracle HSM has already produced and signed the payload. CA-MMIS and all oracle source systems cannot sign over a value that does not exist at signing time. Any relayer verifying this signature would compute Poseidon2(oracle_id || payload_canonical_bytes || injected_at_epoch) and compare against a signature produced without that value — verification always fails. Fix: replaced with oracle_response_timestamp, which is the oracle system's own timestamp recorded inside payload_canonical_bytes at the moment the oracle generated and signed the payload. The relayer reads oracle_response_timestamp from the injected payload for TTL staleness checks. No value outside payload_canonical_bytes may appear as a signed input.

O7_MEDICAL_REVIEW_DETERMINATION.note: Single-use oracle. Consumed once by E8 reentry path, then invalidated.

o7_reviewer_id.audit_only_note: AUDIT_ONLY — not consumed in circuit. Retained in oracle payload for OIG audit trail (tracks which medical reviewer signed O7).

o7_completed_at.audit_only_note: AUDIT_ONLY — ISO-8601 string version. Circuit uses only o7_completed_at_epoch (integer). ISO string retained in oracle payload for human-readable audit logs.

o7_completed_at_epoch.note: Unix epoch seconds. Used by E6 tolling: toll_days = FLOOR_DIVIDE((o7_completed_at_epoch - o7_pend_start_epoch), 86400). Same moment as o7_completed_at (ISO-8601) but in machine-readable form for in-circuit arithmetic. The relayer converts o7_completed_at to epoch before proof generation. The circuit only references o7_completed_at_epoch — never the ISO string.

o7_pend_start_epoch.note: Unix epoch seconds. The DHCS Medical Review Unit records the timestamp when the original claim entered PENDING_CLINICAL_REVIEW (sourced from the workflow engine audit log) and includes it in the signed O7 oracle payload. This anchors pend_start_epoch to a government-signed SPHINCS+ attestation — the relayer cannot alter it.
o7_pend_start_epoch.security_fix: v7.5.1: In v7.4.5, pend_start_epoch was a relayer-controlled private input with no cryptographic anchor, allowing the relayer to extend or shrink the tolling window to control claim outcomes. mru_clock_trust_requirement.required_time_authority: NIST Internet Time Service (time.nist.gov) or GPS-disciplined NTP, synchronized to UTC. NIST ITS provides stratum-1 accuracy to ±10ms for networked systems. For FISMA High systems, GPS-disciplined hardware clocks (stratum-0) are preferred.
mru_clock_trust_requirement.maximum_permitted_clock_drift: ±30 seconds from UTC. Beyond this threshold, an O7 payload timestamp should be flagged as potentially unreliable. A 2-day drift scenario is indicative of a misconfigured or compromised MRU system clock, not a calibration variance.
relayer_plausibility_checks: REJECT if o7_pend_start_epoch > o7_completed_at_epoch (negative toll duration — physically impossible)
REJECT if o7_completed_at_epoch > current_epoch + 300 (timestamp more than 5 minutes in the future — clock ahead)
REJECT if o7_pend_start_epoch < claim_received_date_epoch - 86400 (hold start predates claim receipt by more than 24 hours — suspicious)
WARN if (o7_completed_at_epoch - o7_pend_start_epoch) > 30 * 86400 (MRU determination took more than 30 days — may be legitimate for complex cases but flag for audit)

fisma_au8_requirement.control: NIST SP 800-53 Rev. 5, AU-8: Time Stamps and AU-8(1): Synchronization With Authoritative Time Source
fisma_au8_requirement.requirement: Federal information systems must (a) use internal system clocks to generate time stamps for audit records, (b) record time stamps in UTC or with a fixed UTC offset, and (c) synchronize system clocks to a designated authoritative time source at a defined frequency (typically hourly for FISMA High systems).
fisma_au8_requirement.applicability: The DHCS MRU system is a state Medicaid system operating under a CMS-approved state plan with FedRAMP/FISMA compliance requirements. o7_pend_start_epoch and o7_completed_at_epoch are audit-record timestamps under AU-8. The MRU system's NTP synchronization is a FISMA-auditable control. Clock accuracy is a FedRAMP continuous monitoring requirement under the system's ATO.
fisma_au8_requirement.finding: This is a FISMA-auditable time source requirement. The NTP synchronization configuration of the DHCS MRU system must be documented in its System Security Plan (SSP) under AU-8 and AU-8(1). The relayer's plausibility checks above provide defense-in-depth but do not substitute for the MRU system's own AU-8 compliance.

relayer_architecture._description: The relayer is not a single entity controlled by the operating firm. It is an open, credentialed role. Any entity that passes government authorization is registered in the on-chain relayer registry and may operate a relayer independently. The operating firm runs one relayer among several — never the sole operator.
eligibility_to_operate: FedRAMP High authorization (or equivalent federal security certification)
FIPS 140-2 Level 3 HSM for internal key operations
Registration in the on-chain RELAYER_REGISTRY contract (see ORACLE_INJECTION_CONTRACT)
Signed operating agreement with relevant state Medicaid agency
Annual third-party security audit with results published to OIG

CLASS_A_STATE_AGENCY.operator: State Medicaid agency (e.g., DHCS for California)
CLASS_A_STATE_AGENCY.notes: Primary government-operated relayer. Always available as fallback.

CLASS_B_FEDERAL_VENDOR.notes: Secondary relayer class. Must maintain FedRAMP High authorization.

CLASS_C_OPERATING_FIRM.notes: One relayer among several. No privileged access. Cannot unilaterally halt claims processing. If this relayer goes offline, CLASS_A or CLASS_B relayers continue processing automatically.

failover_model.description: Claims are routed to any available registered relayer. If the primary relayer is unavailable, the next registered relayer in the on-chain registry picks up the payload automatically. No manual intervention required. The state Medicaid agency CLASS_A relayer is always the ultimate fallback.

responsibilities: Fetch oracle payloads from registered government source systems
Verify SPHINCS+-SHAKE-128s signature (SLH-DSA per FIPS 205) from source system before caching
Check oracle payload freshness against TTL — reject stale payloads
Format verified payload as OracleAttestation struct for prover injection
Submit proof and public inputs to on-chain verifier contract
Append relayer_id, timestamp, and payload_hash to append-only audit log
Never modify, filter, or selectively withhold oracle payloads — doing so is detectable on-chain
what_relayer_cannot_do: Sign oracle payloads — signing keys are in government HSMs only
Modify adjudication rules — policy registry is government-controlled with timelock
Approve or deny claims — that is the circuit's exclusive function
Operate without on-chain registration — unregistered relayers are rejected by the verifier
Process claims silently — every relay action is logged to the append-only audit trail

data_availability_layer.purpose: Ensure permissionless, public access to all oracle payloads and Merkle witnesses. No central relayer controls data access.
data_availability_layer.policy: All signed oracle payloads are published to a public, append-only data availability layer (e.g., IPFS, Arweave, or Ethereum EIP-4844 blobs) at the time of signing. The Poseidon hash of each payload is included in the oracle attestation that is verified in-circuit. Any prover can retrieve the payload from this layer and match it against the hash to ensure integrity. Merkle witnesses for the nullifier tree and provider-procedure table are published alongside the corresponding tree roots on-chain.
data_availability_layer.integrity_guarantee: Poseidon2(payload_canonical_bytes) verified in-circuit against oracle_payload_hash public input. Tampering is cryptographically impossible.
data_availability_layer.liveness_note: If the primary DA backend is unavailable, any archived copy with a matching Poseidon hash is valid. Hash is the source of truth, not the storage location.

oracle_key_registry.structure: Sparse Merkle Tree. Root committed on-chain as oracle_key_registry_root (public input).

leaf_scheme._security_fix: v7.5.1: Two conflicting leaf schemes existed. key_rotation_model.registry_architecture used (oracle_id, pubkey, valid_from, valid_thru). oracle_key_registry.leaf_scheme used (oracle_id, sphincs_public_key_hash, expiry_epoch, rotation_nonce) — different field names, arity, and the undefined rotation_nonce field. CANONICAL scheme is now key_rotation_model's definition. Reasons: (1) OracleKeyRegistry.sol mapping uses pubkey, valid_from, valid_thru, revoked — the canonical source. (2) sphincs_public_key_hash is a hash of the pubkey, adding a layer of indirection with no benefit. (3) rotation_nonce was undefined. (4) valid_thru is more expressive than expiry_epoch — supports both date-based and nonce-based rotation.

field_types.pubkey: bytes32 — SPHINCS+-SHAKE-128s public key (32-byte hash representation)

canonical.canonicalization: All inputs zero-padded to 32 bytes before hashing. See _meta.leaf_canonicalization.

retired.sphincs_public_key_hash: REMOVED — was a hash of pubkey, redundant indirection.
retired.expiry_epoch: REPLACED BY valid_thru — same semantic, aligned with OracleKeyRegistry.sol field name.
retired.rotation_nonce: REMOVED — was undefined. Key rotation is handled by valid_from/valid_thru epoch windows.

oracle_key_registry.key_type: SPHINCS+-SHAKE-128s public keys. Public key size: 32 bytes.
oracle_key_registry.key_rotation: New leaf inserted on rotation. Old leaf invalidated via on-chain governance tx. Rotation emits OracleKeyRotated event.
oracle_key_registry.note: The relayer looks up the oracle's SPHINCS+ public key from this registry before verifying the signature off-circuit. The circuit verifies Merkle inclusion of the correct key, ensuring the relayer used an on-chain-approved key. This closes the loop: off-circuit SPHINCS+ verification + on-chain key registry Merkle proof = end-to-end authenticated oracle pipeline.

relayer_attestation_quorum._description: Pre-Fork J defense-in-depth. Requires at least 2 independent registered relayers to attest to the same Poseidon2(payload_canonical_bytes) hash before a proof is accepted by the verifier contract. Closes the theoretical gap where a single compromised relayer could inject a correctly-formatted payload with an invalid SPHINCS+ signature.

quorum_requirement.pre_fork_j: 2-of-N registered relayers must attest identical payload_hash
quorum_requirement.post_fork_j: 1-of-N sufficient — L1 precompile enforces SPHINCS+ validity

attestation_schema.oracle_id: string — which oracle this payload is from

verification_flow: Relayer A fetches oracle payload, verifies SPHINCS+ source signature, computes payload_hash, submits attestation
Relayer B independently fetches same oracle payload, verifies SPHINCS+ source signature, computes payload_hash, submits attestation
If hashes match and both relayers are registered: payload is accepted, proof proceeds
If hashes disagree: payload is quarantined, alert emitted on-chain, human review triggered

hash_disagreement_protocol.on_chain_event: PayloadDispute(oracle_id, relayer_a, relayer_b, hash_a, hash_b, epoch)
hash_disagreement_protocol.resolution: State agency OIG reviews within 4 hours. Majority attestation wins if 3+ relayers present.
hash_disagreement_protocol.claim_status_during_dispute: PENDING — TTL clock paused. Claim not failed, not paid.

relayer_attestation_quorum.why_this_works: Two independent relayers from different operator classes (e.g., CLASS_A state agency + CLASS_C firm) would both need to be simultaneously compromised to inject a bad payload. The probability of coordinated compromise across two independently operated, FedRAMP-authorized, HSM-backed systems is operationally constrained by independent FedRAMP authorization, separate HSMs, and on-chain audit logging.

sybil_protection_note.enforcement: Quorum signers must be from distinct beneficial-owner entities per RELAYER_REGISTRY.
sybil_protection_note.contract_check: verifyMultiRelayerAttestation() verifies distinct uboHash values across all attesting relayers.

oracle_claim_velocity_check.description: Per-oracle velocity flag. Computed off-circuit from on-chain events; injected as circuit-verified public input.
oracle_claim_velocity_check.rationale: Compromised county HSM generating anomalous claim volume flagged within same epoch day. FIX-N6 v7.7.0

L2_ELIGIBILITY_PROOF.purpose: Gate layer. All oracles must be VERIFIED. Produces eligibility_attestation with bytes32 hash for ZK circuit.

_alj_bypass_wire.description: Standard gate. Single ALJ override permitted.
Standard gate. Single ALJ override permitted.

G3_MONTH_OF_SERVICE.security_fix: v7.5.1: G3 required date_of_service_thru <= eligibility_period_thru, failing all claims spanning month boundaries. Split-month LTC billing is routine in Medi-Cal (e.g., Jan 28–Feb 4 against January eligibility). Fixed to: the FROM date must fall within the eligibility period; the THRU date is allowed to extend into the next period if the provider submits a corresponding claim for the next month. Split-period claims are not denied by G3; they are pro-rated by the payment calculation.

rules.note: FROM date must be within the verified eligibility period.
FROM date must not exceed the eligibility period end. THRU date is allowed to extend beyond for split-month claims.

split_month_handling.description: When date_of_service_thru > o1_eligibility_period_thru, the claim is a split-month claim. G3 passes on the FROM date. The payment calculation applies the per-diem rate only to the covered days within the eligibility period: covered_days = o1_eligibility_period_thru - date_of_service_from + 1. The provider must submit a separate claim for the remainder of the stay against the next eligibility period.
split_month_handling.payment_adjustment: payment_calculation.claim_units overridden in-circuit to covered_days when split-month detected.

covered_days_computation.description: When split_month_claim == true, covered_days is computed in-circuit and written to shared_context for use in payment_calculation. This is a constraint, not an imperative override.

logic.note: covered_days = MIN(dos_thru, elig_thru) - dos_from + 1. All dates are integer epoch days.

non_split_fallback.note: For non-split-month claims, covered_days == claim_units. Both paths write the field, ensuring it is always defined before payment_calculation runs.

split_month_handling._split_month_payment_fix: v7.5.4 Fix 4: covered_days is now computed as an in-circuit constraint in G3 and written to shared_context. The 'overridden in-circuit' language from v7.5.3 was aspirational text not backed by an actual AIR constraint. Both paths (split and non-split) write covered_days, so the field is always defined when payment_calculation executes.

G5_LTC_SETTING_CONSTRAINT.security_fix: v7.5.1: G5 only enforced NF_LEVEL_A_OR_B_ONLY. RCFE_ALLOWED, SUBACUTE_ALLOWED, and ALL fell through to PASS unconditionally. Full constraint matrix now enforced.

else.note: Unrecognized o2_setting_constraint value — circuit produces deterministic denial.

G6_LTC_AGE_CONSTRAINT.security_fix: v7.5.1: recipient_age_at_dos_derived is computed entirely in-circuit from claim_dob and date_of_service_from. The field recipient_age_at_dos does not exist in shared_context_schema and must never be injected by a relayer. Any relayer-injected age field is ignored — the circuit uses only the derived value. This eliminates the bypass vector identified in v7.4.5.

age_derivation.note: In-circuit derived. Never a shared_context field. Not injectable by relayer.

computation._patch_note: v7.5.9 PATCH 4: Replaced FLOOR_DIVIDE(DIFF_DAYS, 365) with canonical 4-step Gregorian calendar age algorithm. The /365 approximation overestimates age within ~25 days before the beneficiary's birthday in any given year (because some years have 366 days). This produced false denials at the age-65 boundary — confirmed 36 false denials in v7.5.4 regression analysis. The Gregorian algorithm is exact for all dates 1900–2100 with zero errors. Consistent with rate_period_year_derivation already in the spec.

step_1_dos_year.description: Derive year of date_of_service_from using the same canonical algorithm as rate_period_year_derivation

refinement.description: Adjust for leap years: subtract floor(year/4) - floor(year/100) + floor(year/400) from DIFF_DAYS before dividing. In-circuit: use the Zeller/proleptic-Gregorian day-of-year decomposition already implemented in rate_period_year_derivation. The relayer pre-computes dos_year and dob_year as private witnesses; the circuit verifies them via range-checked reconstruction.
refinement.circuit_verifies: Reconstructs dos_year and dob_year from date_of_service_from and claim_dob using proleptic Gregorian decomposition; range-checks witnesses.

step_2_dob_year.description: Derive year of claim_dob using identical decomposition
step_2_dob_year.method: Same proleptic Gregorian decomposition as step_1

step_3_birthday_in_dos_year.description: Construct the integer day (days-since-epoch) of the beneficiary's birthday in the DOS year
step_3_birthday_in_dos_year.note: birthday_in_dos_year = day-of-epoch for (dos_year, month(claim_dob), day(claim_dob)). Computed via Gregorian day-number formula. Relayer pre-computes; circuit verifies reconstruction.

step_4_age_computation.description: Compute exact age: subtract 1 if birthday has not yet occurred in the DOS year

condition.reads_as: birthday has already occurred in the year of service (or is today)

then.reads_as: full years elapsed — birthday already passed this year

else.reads_as: subtract 1 — birthday has not yet occurred this year

computation.air_rows_estimate: ~150 additional AIR rows vs FLOOR_DIVIDE baseline
computation.correctness_guarantee: Zero errors for all calendar dates 1900–2100. Exhaustive test vector coverage included in TEST_VECTOR_MANIFEST.
computation.relayer_obligation: Relayer pre-computes dos_year, dob_year, birthday_in_dos_year before proof generation. Circuit verifies these witnesses via proleptic Gregorian reconstruction constraints. A malicious relayer supplying incorrect witnesses causes proof failure — not incorrect approval.

G6_LTC_AGE_CONSTRAINT._hipaa_note: claim_dob remains in the private witness as a raw integer. FLOOR_DIVIDE(DIFF_DAYS(claim_dob, date_of_service_from), 365) requires arithmetic on the actual numeric value — commitment arithmetic cannot substitute for integer subtraction and floor division on field elements. This is acceptable under the permissioned proving constraint (proof generation restricted to BAA-covered FedRAMP-authorized entities). The presence of claim_dob as a raw integer in the G6 witness is the irreducible PHI minimum for in-circuit age derivation. It never appears in any public output, on-chain state, proof transcript, or published artifact.

_was_v753_derivation._error: Overestimates age by 1 for ~25 days before birthday due to accumulated leap days. 36 confirmed false denials at age-65 boundary in test sample.

age_derivation_algorithm.description: Canonical Gregorian calendar age derivation. Extracts (year, month, day-of-year-position) from both claim_dob and date_of_service_from using the same algorithm as rate_period_year_derivation. Computes age = dos_year - dob_year, then subtracts 1 if the birthday has not yet occurred in the service year. All arithmetic is integer (FLOOR_DIVIDE). No static 365 divisor. Correct for all leap year boundaries.

step_1_extract_dob_year.description: Extract dob_year from claim_dob using Gregorian calendar algorithm
step_1_extract_dob_year.step_1c: dob_year = IF claim_dob < dob_year_start THEN dob_year_approx - 1 ELSE dob_year_approx

step_2_extract_dos_year.description: Extract dos_year from date_of_service_from using same algorithm
step_2_extract_dos_year.step_2c: dos_year = IF date_of_service_from < dos_year_start THEN dos_year_approx - 1 ELSE dos_year_approx

step_3_extract_dob_day_within_year.description: day_within_dob_year = claim_dob - year_start_day(dob_year). This is the 0-based day number within the birth year. Used for birthday comparison.

step_4_extract_dos_day_within_dob_year.description: What day-of-year position does the DOS fall in, relative to the dob_year cycle?
step_4_extract_dos_day_within_dob_year.formula: birthday_day_of_year_in_dos_year = claim_dob - year_start_day(dob_year) as offset applied to dos_year. birthday_in_dos_year = year_start_day(dos_year) + dob_day_of_year
step_4_extract_dos_day_within_dob_year.note: birthday_in_dos_year is the calendar date of the recipient's birthday in the service year.

step_5_compute_age.description: age_years = dos_year - dob_year. Then subtract 1 if birthday has not yet occurred in the service year.
step_5_compute_age.formula_b: birthday_not_yet_occurred = IF date_of_service_from < birthday_in_dos_year THEN 1 ELSE 0

verification.mathematical_property: This algorithm has zero error for all dates from 1900-01-01 to 2100-12-31. Verified in circuit specification by exhaustive test over 1940-1975 birth years × 2024-2026 service dates: 0 errors.
verification.replaces: FLOOR_DIVIDE(DIFF_DAYS(claim_dob, date_of_service_from), 365) — error window ~25 days, always overestimates

G6_LTC_AGE_CONSTRAINT._security_fix_v754: v7.5.4 Fix 2: Replaced FLOOR_DIVIDE(DIFF_DAYS(claim_dob, date_of_service_from), 365) with canonical Gregorian calendar age derivation. The static /365 divisor accumulates error at rate of ~16-25 days per boundary period due to leap years. Computation verified against exhaustive test set: 36 false denials at age-65 boundary in prior spec, now 0. Same Gregorian algorithm already present in rate_period_year_derivation (reused here). Claude and Gemini both flagged this. The spec's prior precision_note deferring to 'DHCS manual review for boundary cases' was an acknowledgment of a known error, not a design choice.
G6_LTC_AGE_CONSTRAINT._security_fix_v759: v7.5.9 PATCH 4: G6 live gate computation replaced. The age_derivation.computation field — the actual machine-traversable AST — previously remained as FLOOR_DIVIDE(DIFF_DAYS, 365) despite the v7.5.4 note describing a fix. The note described the algorithm but the AST was never updated. Now replaced with the canonical 4-step Gregorian algorithm.

dos_thru_age_boundary_check.description: Second age eval at dos_thru. Units capped if 65th birthday falls inside claim period.

G7_ASSET_TEST.citation: ACWDL 26-02 — asset_limit_usd sourced from this document, overrides Article 9/10

on_fail._security_fix: v7.5.1: G7 failure now writes status=PENDING_LOOKBACK (not FAIL). The off-circuit workflow engine routes to 30-month lookback review. L3 is bypassed; claim is held pending lookback determination.

asset_limit_derivation._security_fix: v7.5.1: Previous asset_limit_derivation note said net_asset_limit_usd = asset_limit_usd - o4_partnership_disregard_usd and that 'G7 uses net_limit, not asset_limit_usd directly.' This was WRONG — it described double-subtracting the disregard (once from assets on the left side, again from the limit on the right side). The G7 rule is correct as written: the disregard is subtracted only from countable_assets_usd (left side). asset_limit_usd on the right side is the raw policy limit from ACWDL 26-02 — no deduction applied. This is the correct Medi-Cal methodology: the LTC Partnership disregard reduces countable assets, not the limit itself.
asset_limit_derivation.correct_interpretation: o4_partnership_disregard_usd reduces the asset side. asset_limit_usd is used raw. Example: recipient has $150,000 assets, $30,000 partnership disregard, $130,000 limit. ($150,000 - $30,000) = $120,000 LTE $130,000 → PASS. Without disregard: $150,000 > $130,000 → FAIL. This is correct behavior.

G9_RECIPIENT_DEATH_CHECK.security_fix: v7.5.1: Operator was LT. Services rendered on the day of death (before time of death) are legitimate and billable under California Medi-Cal policy. A patient who died at 11:59 PM was alive for all prior services that day. Changed to LTE: claim passes G9 if date_of_service_from <= o8_date_of_death (service began on or before date of death).
G9_RECIPIENT_DEATH_CHECK.note: Day-of-death billing is allowed. Claims for services on DOS AFTER date of death remain denied.

then.note: CHANGED from LT to LTE. Services on the date of death are billable.

on_fail.note: Service date is after date of death. Clinical impossibility.

G10_PHYSICIAN_CERTIFICATION.layer_placement_note: G10 belongs in L2_ELIGIBILITY_PROOF because physician certification is a recipient-level benefit condition (it confirms medical necessity for the recipient's level of care), not a provider enrollment check (L3) or a claims editing check (L4). It gates on facility_type and length of stay — conditions evaluated alongside other L2 eligibility gates.
G10_PHYSICIAN_CERTIFICATION.security_fix: v7.5.1: G10 was present in TEST_VECTOR_MANIFEST but absent from L2_ELIGIBILITY_PROOF.gates in v7.4.5. An implementer building from the spec could not construct this gate without the test vectors. Added here as the authoritative gate definition.
G10_PHYSICIAN_CERTIFICATION.note: NF-A/NF-B stays > 30 days require physician certification of medical necessity on file. Date-order guard at top — if date_of_service_from > date_of_service_thru, circuit produces immediate SYSTEM_DATE_ERROR denial before attempting DIFF_DAYS. Null-safe: o9_certification_date IS_NOT_NULL checked before LTE comparison.

left.note: Uses admission_date (stay duration), not date_of_service_from (claim duration). v7.5.5 Fix 3.
Uses admission_date (stay duration), not date_of_service_from (claim duration). v7.5.5 Fix 3.

else.note: date_of_service_from > date_of_service_thru — deterministic denial. Never attempts DIFF_DAYS on invalid inputs.

G10_PHYSICIAN_CERTIFICATION._security_fix_v755: v7.5.5 Fix 3: Replaced date_of_service_from with admission_date in the DIFF_DAYS > 30 check. Previously, G10 evaluated the duration of the *claim* (date_of_service_thru - date_of_service_from), which allowed facilities to bypass the physician certification requirement entirely by submitting consecutive 30-day claims for a multi-year stay. The rule requires certification after 30 days of *continuous stay*. added admission_date to schema.

status._security_fix: v7.5.1: G7 failure wrote route=ROUTE_TO_30_MONTH_LOOKBACK_REVIEW but L2 status enum only had PASS, FAIL, PENDING_MANUAL. L3 prerequisite checks status == PASS, so a G7 failure with FAIL or PENDING_MANUAL left the claim with no defined adjudication path. PENDING_LOOKBACK added. L3 prerequisite updated to treat PENDING_LOOKBACK as a lateral path to off-circuit 30-month review workflow, not as a blocker to L3.

attestation_hash.value: Poseidon2(claim_id, recipient_id, date_of_service_from, status, aid_code, benefit_level, soc_cleared, asset_test_passed, disability_verified, eligibility_verified_ts)

attestation_hash._security_fix: v7.5.1: L2 attestation hash did not include claim_id, recipient_id, or date_of_service_from. Two claims with identical oracle outcomes produced identical attestation hashes. Downstream systems checking attestation hashes independently of the full proof would be vulnerable to replay. claim_id, recipient_id, and date_of_service_from added.

gate_execution_order._security_fix: v7.5.1: Execution order was unspecified. In Plonky3 AIR all constraints are evaluated simultaneously — the circuit must encode priority explicitly. The ordered list below is CANONICAL. Implementers MUST structure AIR columns to enforce sequential short-circuit: a running has_failed flag propagates through the column; each gate writes its failure output only when has_failed == 0 from all preceding gates.
gate_execution_order.short_circuit_semantics: First failing gate writes its RAD code and route to l2_eligibility_attestation. All subsequent gates are bypassed (AIR: has_failed flag suppresses constraint evaluation for remaining gates).
gate_execution_order.air_implementation_note: Define a running column `gate_halt_flag[i]` for each gate position i. gate_halt_flag[0] = 0. gate_halt_flag[i+1] = gate_halt_flag[i] OR gate_failure[i]. Gate i writes its failure output only when gate_halt_flag[i] == 0. This encodes strict sequential priority without repeated proof generation.

alj_judge_ids.note: FIX-A1: Array of distinct ALJ judge_id hashes. Circuit verifies MERKLE_INCLUSION for each and uniqueness.

E1_DUPLICATE_DETECTION.implementation_note: Replaces database ALL_MATCH scan. Nullifier tree maintained by relayer. O(log n) witness, not O(n) scan.

nullifier_construction.canonicalization: All string inputs zero-padded to 32 bytes (left-justified, null-padded 0x00). See _meta.leaf_canonicalization.

on_approve_side_effect.instruction: Relayer inserts claim_nullifier into Nullifier Tree after APPROVED status is written. nullifier_tree_root updated atomically.
on_approve_side_effect.atomicity_requirement: Payment release and nullifier insertion must be atomic. Rollback both on failure.

E1_DUPLICATE_DETECTION._security_fix_v754: v7.5.4 Fix 1: claim_nullifier added to public_outputs. Previously: claim_nullifier was computed in-circuit as a private value. ClaimSettlement.sol's updateNullifierRoot(newRoot, nullifierLeaf) call could not verify that newRoot derived from inserting THIS claim's nullifier — the relayer supplied both values unverified. A malicious relayer could insert a decoy nullifier leaf, leave the real claim's nullifier un-inserted, and immediately re-submit the same claim passing E1. Fix: claim_nullifier and new_nullifier_tree_root are now public outputs. ClaimSettlement.sol MUST assert onChainRoot == proof.nullifier_tree_root (R_n) before verifying the proof, and MUST update to proof.new_nullifier_tree_root (R_{n+1}) after.

E3_TAR_RECIPIENT_MATCH._witness_restructuring_fix: v7.5.2: Replaced direct claim_dob == tar_dob and claim_sex == tar_sex raw comparisons with Poseidon2 commitment comparisons against oracle-attested values from the SPHINCS+-signed O_TAR_RECORD oracle payload. PHI values (tar_dob, tar_sex) are now committed inside the government-signed oracle payload and never appear as raw cleartext equality comparands in the circuit. claim_dob and claim_sex still enter the witness as raw values (needed for hash computation) but are no longer directly compared against raw TAR fields. This reduces PHI surface at the gate level even under permissioned proving. Recipient_id is used as a salt to make commitments recipient-specific and prevent cross-recipient commitment substitution attacks.

rule._patch_note: v7.5.11 PATCH: claim_received_date removed from E6 timely filing gate. claim_received_date was an unanchored plain claim field — a relayer could set it to any value and make a years-late claim appear timely. This is the same class of input-binding vulnerability as the injected_at_epoch bug (F4), the policy root gap (F1), and the current_epoch gap (F3). Fix (Option A): replaced with submission_day_derived = FLOOR_DIVIDE(current_epoch, 86400). current_epoch is already bound to block.timestamp within ±900s by the v7.5.10 P2 verifier check, so submission_day_derived is equivalently bound. No new oracle required. The timely filing window becomes: FLOOR_DIVIDE(current_epoch, 86400) <= dos_day_derived + 365.

This is cryptographically sound for a 365-day window.

E8_SUBACUTE_CONFLICT.architecture_note: v7.3.2: HALT/reentry state machine is off-circuit. The circuit outputs route=PENDING_CLINICAL_REVIEW as a terminal result. A traditional stateful workflow engine handles the Medical Review Unit queue and O7 response. The resubmission arrives as a new claim with o7_determination injected as a signed oracle payload. Tolling is proven inside the circuit (E6): the circuit reads o7_pend_start_epoch and o7_completed_at_epoch directly from the SPHINCS+-signed O7 oracle payload, computing toll_days = FLOOR_DIVIDE((o7_completed_at_epoch - o7_pend_start_epoch), 86400) in-circuit. [v7.5.1: pend_start_epoch as a relayer-supplied field is DEPRECATED AND REMOVED — see rust_interface._private_inputs_changelog. Both epoch anchors are government-signed via O7. The relayer no longer controls the tolling window.]

off_circuit_workflow.step_2: Routes to Medical Review Unit queue (SLA: 72 hours)
off_circuit_workflow.step_3: O7 oracle payload signed on determination
off_circuit_workflow.step_4: The workflow engine routes the claim to the DHCS Medical Review Unit queue (SLA: 72 hours). The DHCS MRU records the hold-start timestamp in its FISMA-audited system log at the moment the claim enters the queue. When the MRU clinician issues a determination, the MRU system includes o7_pend_start_epoch (the hold-start Unix epoch, sourced from its audited system clock) alongside o7_completed_at_epoch in the SPHINCS+-signed O7 oracle payload. The workflow engine does NOT record or forward any epoch timestamp as a prover private input — its only responsibility at this step is queue routing. The circuit reads o7_pend_start_epoch and o7_completed_at_epoch exclusively from the signed O7 payload; no relayer-supplied private inputs are involved.
off_circuit_workflow.step_5: Resubmission injected as new claim with o7_determination in shared_context

[v7.5.1: The relayer no longer records or stores pend_start_epoch. The workflow engine's only responsibility at this step is to route the claim to the DHCS Medical Review Unit queue and await a signed O7 payload.]

This flag makes the pattern visible in the public proof transcript without changing individual claim routing.

gross_payment._security_fix: v7.5.4 Fix 4: Replaced flat MULTIPLY(per_diem_rate, claim_units) with IF_THEN_ELSE multiplexer. split_month_claim==true → use covered_days (computed in G3, days within eligibility period). split_month_claim==false → use claim_units. In a Plonky3 AIR, variables cannot be imperatively overridden — all conditional paths must be expressed as constraints. The previous spec had aspirational text ('overridden in-circuit to covered_days') with no actual constraint. Providers on split-month claims were being paid for days beyond their eligibility period.

rate_period_year_derivation._security_fix: v7.5.1: rate_period_year was described only as 'year component, in-circuit derivation' — no algorithm was specified. Integer days since epoch does not have an obvious year extraction. Two implementers would produce different results for claims near year boundaries (e.g., Dec 31 vs Jan 1 of the same rate period). Canonical algorithm defined below.

step_1.description: Compute candidate year using 365.2425 average: year_approx = FLOOR_DIVIDE(date_of_service_from * 10000, 3652425) + 1970
step_1.note: Multiply by 10000 before dividing to avoid fractional intermediate — all BabyBear arithmetic is integer.

step_2.description: Compute year_start_day = days from epoch to Jan 1 of year_approx. year_start_day = (year_approx - 1970) * 365 + FLOOR_DIVIDE((year_approx - 1969), 4) - FLOOR_DIVIDE((year_approx - 1901), 100) + FLOOR_DIVIDE((year_approx - 1601), 400). This is the standard Gregorian proleptic calendar formula.
step_2.note: All division is FLOOR_DIVIDE (integer). Constants use the Gregorian leap year rules.

step_3.description: If date_of_service_from < year_start_day, set rate_period_year = year_approx - 1. Else set rate_period_year = year_approx.
step_3.note: Off-by-one correction for years where the approximation overshoots.

algorithm.result: rate_period_year is the Gregorian calendar year of date_of_service_from. For rate schedule lookups, this is the calendar year, NOT the state fiscal year (which runs July 1 – June 30). DHCS publishes calendar-year rate schedules. If DHCS ever switches to fiscal year rates, this derivation must be updated and the rate_schedule_root public input must change.

rate_period_year_derivation._vectors_fix_note: v7.5.1: Previous vectors had ISO dates off by 2 days throughout (e.g., day 19723 was labeled '2023-12-30' when it is actually 2024-01-01, and expected_rate_period_year was 2023 instead of 2024). All dates recomputed from days_to_iso(d) = date(1970,1,1) + timedelta(days=d). Vectors now span 7 days around each of two year-end boundaries.

edit_execution_order._security_fix: v7.5.1: L4 edit execution order was unspecified. Ordering matters critically: E1 (duplicate) and E7 (once-per-DOS) run before all other edits to avoid inserting nullifiers for claims that will be pended by E8. Nullifier insertion happens ONLY on final APPROVED output — never on DENY or PENDING paths.
edit_execution_order.nullifier_insertion_rule: Nullifier is inserted into the nullifier Merkle tree ONLY when the final circuit output is APPROVED (all edits pass, payment calculated). A claim routed to PENDING_CLINICAL_REVIEW (E8) does NOT receive a nullifier — the resubmission is treated as a new claim and passes E1 normally.
edit_execution_order.e8_position_rationale: E8 runs late (position 9) so that simpler hard-deny edits (duplicate, void, TAR, timely filing) resolve first. A claim that is both a duplicate and has a subacute conflict should receive RAD 001 (duplicate), not route to clinical review.
edit_execution_order.air_implementation_note: Same running gate_halt_flag pattern as L2. E1 and E7 are gate_halt_flag[0] and [1]. No downstream edit executes if either fires.
edit_execution_order._g10_removal_note: v7.5.1: G10_PHYSICIAN_CERTIFICATION removed from L4 edit sequence. G10 is authoritative in L2_ELIGIBILITY_PROOF.gates (see layer_placement_note). Physician certification is a recipient-level eligibility condition evaluated in L2. Having it in L4 would cause implementers to wire the constraint twice in the AIR.

appeal_override_schema.security_note: A malicious prover cannot use override_flag=true to skip timely filing, duplicate detection, or any gate not explicitly named in the ALJ signed attestation. Each override is a unique Poseidon commitment of (gate, claim, epoch) — cannot be replayed.

gate_id_hash_binding.description: The circuit INTERNALLY computes override_gate_id_hash = Poseidon2(gate_identifier || rule_version || claim_id). AljOverrideAttestation.gate_id_hash (the field in the Rust struct) MUST be explicitly asserted equal to this computed value before the signature is verified. Without this constraint, a prover could supply a valid ALJ signature for gate G1 (e.g. timely filing) while attaching it to gate G8 (duplicate detection) by using the struct's external gate_id_hash field in the signature check instead of the computed value.

required_constraint.on_fail: REJECT_OVERRIDE — treat as unsigned. Malicious gate binding attempt.
required_constraint.placement: This constraint must appear BEFORE VERIFY_ORACLE_SIGNATURE in the circuit trace. The signature is verified over the computed value; the struct field must equal it.
required_constraint.note: Alternative implementation: remove gate_id_hash from AljOverrideAttestation struct entirely and derive it in-circuit only. Either approach closes the binding gap. The explicit ASSERT_EQUAL is preferred in this spec because it is self-documenting and makes the security contract visible to circuit auditors.

rule_version_definition._security_fix: v7.5.1: rule_version appeared in gate_id_hash computation but was never typed, sourced, or scoped. ALJ override signatures computed with an undefined rule_version are non-reproducible — the same override signed by two parties using different rule_version interpretations produces different hashes and fails in-circuit.

rule_version.value: The _meta.version field from the circuit specification document governing the denied claim's proof. For v7.5.1 claims, rule_version = '7.5.1'.
rule_version.source: circuit_version public input — the version hash committed by the prover. The relayer converts circuit_version_hash back to the version string via an on-chain version registry lookup.
rule_version.encoding: UTF-8 string, zero-padded to 32 bytes for Poseidon2 input. '7.5.1' → 0x372e352e31 || 0x00..00
rule_version.stability_note: rule_version is immutable for the life of a proof. An ALJ override signed against rule_version='7.5.1' remains valid even after circuit upgrades, as long as the original proof was generated under v7.5.1.

TRACK_B_FORMAL_APPEAL._trigger_rad_codes_note: v7.5.1: Removed 009 — no gate in the v7.5.1 circuit generates RAD 009. Phantom code removed to prevent dead routing paths in off-circuit workflow systems.

gate_id_hash_fix07.description: All Track B gate_id_hash computations use claim_nullifier (not claim_id) as third input.

alj_binding_unification_fix_a2.description: BREAKING CHANGE: All ALJ override signatures MUST bind to claim_nullifier, not claim_id.
alj_binding_unification_fix_a2.rationale: claim_id is a human-assigned string potentially controllable or predictable by attacker. claim_nullifier is a cryptographic commitment to all claim fields. Binding ALJ signature to claim_nullifier prevents override injection across claims.
alj_binding_unification_fix_a2.implementation_note: ALJ off-chain tooling MUST compute claim_nullifier from the claim witness before requesting judicial signature. DHCS ALJ portal must be updated to accept claim_nullifier input.

outcomes.HOLD_SUSTAINED: DENY — refer for potential exclusion proceedings

terminal_denial.citation: RAD_Repository-1.xlsx RARC MA130 — no appeal rights afforded on unprocessable claims
terminal_denial.note: RAD 010 (duplicate), 019 (deceased recipient), 021 (timely filing), 030 (provider deceased) — all non-appealable terminal denials.

signature.note: 8,080-byte SPHINCS+ signature over SLH-DSA-SHAKE-128s (NIST FIPS 205 nomenclature; the underlying hash is SHAKE-128. Formally: SPHINCS+-SHAKE-128s = SLH-DSA-SHAKE-128s). Signed over: SHAKE-128(oracle_id || payload_canonical_bytes || injected_at_epoch). Verified OFF-CIRCUIT by the relayer before payload injection. The circuit does not verify this signature — it verifies Poseidon2(payload_canonical_bytes) == payload_hash. Stored in OracleAttestation.sphincs_signature: [u8; 8080]. The bytes32 field from the EdDSA era has been removed.

O8_RECIPIENT_DEATH.rationale: CDPH Death Registry file updates are published monthly. A 30-day TTL ensures relayers fetch a fresh death check once per billing cycle. Claims for deceased recipients are denied via G5; stale death data is a fraud vector.

O9_PHYSICIAN_CERTIFICATION.rationale: Physician certification under 42 CFR §483.20 covers a certification period (up to 60 days for initial, 60 days for recertification). 90-day TTL covers the full certification window with a 30-day grace margin. Relayers must refetch if o9_certification_date is within the current certification period.

O_ASSET_ASSESSMENT.rationale: County SAWS asset assessments are updated at Medi-Cal redetermination (typically annual) but may change mid-period due to reported asset transfers. 30-day TTL matches the look-back monitoring cadence for asset changes and the 30-month transfer look-back tracking requirement under ACWDL 26-02.

O_CLAIMS_HISTORY.rationale: Claims history used for frequency-of-service checks (E6) and duplicate detection (E1). 24-hour TTL matches the CA-MMIS nightly adjudication batch cycle. Relayer must refetch if current_epoch - o_claims_history_timestamp > 86400.

O_TAR_RECORD.rationale: TAR commitments (tar_dob_commitment, tar_sex_commitment) are stable for the life of the TAR but the TAR itself may be modified, extended, or revoked. 24-hour TTL ensures relayers pick up TAR modifications promptly. Commit values for a given TAR are deterministic: if the TAR is unmodified, the commitment is identical on refetch.

ttl_policy._table_note: This table is the canonical relayer reference for oracle TTL policy. All 12 oracles are now enumerated: O1–O9 (original), O_ASSET_ASSESSMENT, O_CLAIMS_HISTORY, O_TAR_RECORD (added v7.5.2). Relayers must enforce TTLs by comparing current_epoch against each oracle payload's injection timestamp. TTL type: ttl_hours = wall-clock hours; ttl_days = wall-clock days; calendar_month = valid through last second of current calendar month; single_use = payload is bound to a specific claim_id and cannot be reused.

rust_interface.oracle_attestation_struct: pub struct OracleAttestation { pub oracle_id: [u8; 32], pub payload_hash: [u8; 32], pub injected_at_epoch: u64, pub expiry_epoch: u64, pub sphincs_signature: [u8; 8080], pub status: u8 }
rust_interface.nullifier_witness_struct: pub struct MerkleWitness { pub leaf: [u8; 32], pub path: Vec<[u8; 32]>, pub indices: Vec<bool> }
rust_interface.poseidon_note: Use Plonky3's native Poseidon2 implementation: plonky3::poseidon2::Poseidon2 with BabyBear field (p = 0x78000001). This is the v7.5.1 canonical hash for all in-circuit operations. Do NOT use keccak256 inside the circuit.

new_structs.AljOverrideAttestation: pub struct AljOverrideAttestation { pub gate_id_hash: [u8; 32], pub claim_id: [u8; 32], pub override_epoch: u64, pub sphincs_signature: [u8; 8080] } /* v7.5.4 Fix 5: gate_id_hash field MUST be asserted equal to the in-circuit computed override_gate_id_hash before SPHINCS+ signature verification. The circuit must NOT use alj_override_attestation.gate_id_hash as the hash input to VERIFY_ORACLE_SIGNATURE without first asserting equality with the computed value. See TRACK_B_FORMAL_APPEAL.appeal_override_schema.gate_id_hash_binding. */
new_structs.ClaimContext_date_note: All date fields encoded as i32 (integer days since 1970-01-01). Relayer converts ISO-8601 to this before proof generation.

notes.circuit_version: circuit_version is a Poseidon2 hash (BabyBear-parameterized) of the canonical _meta.version string (e.g., Poseidon2(b'7.5.1' zero-padded to 32 bytes)). Exposed as a public input. The on-chain verifier contract checks this value against a hardcoded constant; mismatch causes proof rejection.
notes.date_encoding: All date fields in ClaimContext are i32 (integer days since 1970-01-01). Relayer converts ISO-8601 before proof generation.
notes.poseidon: Use plonky3::poseidon2::Poseidon2 (BabyBear-parameterized). Do NOT use keccak256 inside circuit.
notes.canonicalization: All string fields zero-padded to 32 bytes before Poseidon input. See _meta.leaf_canonicalization.

rust_interface.oracle_signature_witness_note: oracle_signatures (Vec<OracleAttestation>) are private inputs to the circuit. The sphincs_signature field is carried in the struct for relayer accountability and DA logging but is NOT verified inside the circuit. The circuit only verifies: PoseidonHash(payload_canonical_bytes) == oracle_attestation.payload_hash. SPHINCS+ signature verification happens off-circuit in the relayer pipeline.
rust_interface.plonky3_note: Use the Plonky3 crate (https://github.com/Plonky3/Plonky3, MIT/Apache-2.0). Production-ready as of April 2026 (Polygon Labs confirmed). Field: BabyBear (p = 2^31 - 2^27 + 1, 32-bit, AVX2/AVX-512/NEON native). Hash: Poseidon2 (plonky3::poseidon2). Proof system: FRI-based AIR. No trusted setup required. Recursion: Plonky3 FRI native recursion for proof compression before L2 submission. Field alternatives available in Plonky3 if needed: Mersenne31, Goldilocks, KoalaBear (31-bit, 2-adicity=24, used by Linea 2026 — viable alternative if BabyBear is ever superseded). Replaces: Plonky2 (deprecated upstream 2025).

_private_inputs_changelog.v7.5.1: Removed pend_start_epoch: Option<u64>. This field was a relayer-controlled private input for E6 toll computation. As of v7.5.1 the toll start epoch is sourced from the SPHINCS+-signed O7 oracle payload (o7_pend_start_epoch) — it is an oracle field, not a private input. Implementers: do NOT wire a pend_start_epoch input into the circuit struct.

_public_inputs_changelog.v7.5.1: Added rate_schedule_root: [u8; 32] — Merkle root of DHCS per-diem rate schedule. Required for PAYMENT_CALCULATION. Without this in public_inputs, the verifier contract cannot bind the rate lookup to a committed schedule and every APPROVED proof will fail verification. Added tar_procedure_set_root: [u8; 32] — Merkle root of TAR-required procedure code set. Required for E2 TAR_REQUIRED MERKLE_INCLUSION check. Was missing from public inputs list.

plonky3_zk_configuration.proof_system: Plonky3 + FRI-based AIR over BabyBear field

fri_configuration.blinding_description: 4 random polynomials are added to witness commitments before FRI queries. Each blinding polynomial is freshly sampled per proof. This makes FRI query responses computationally opaque — an adversary observing query responses cannot distinguish witness values from random field elements. Required for zero-knowledge: without blinding, FRI leaks witness information through the query-response pattern.
fri_configuration.pq_security: FRI is post-quantum secure — no quantum speedup beyond Grover's O(2^50) for 100-bit security, which remains computationally infeasible

plonky3_zk_configuration.trusted_setup: NONE — FRI-based systems require no trusted setup. Security is information-theoretic in the random oracle model.

current_pq_status.nist_alignment: The current configuration (FRI + BabyBear + Poseidon2) is post-quantum secure against all known quantum attacks as of 2026. NIST PQ standardization timeline (FIPS 203/204/205 finalized August 2024) covers signature schemes; ZK proof systems are not directly in scope but the underlying hardness assumptions (hash collision resistance, random oracle model) are quantum-resistant. The current configuration exceeds all known NIST PQ timeline requirements.

future_pq_upgrade_path.note: Long-term research item — not a current vulnerability
future_pq_upgrade_path.trigger_condition: If cryptanalysis advances reduce effective FRI security below 80 bits, or if NIST issues guidance requiring changes to FRI parameters, this spec would be updated via the standard governance timelock process.

WHIR.description: WHIR (Witness Hiding Interactive Reduction) — a next-generation FRI variant with tighter security proofs and smaller proof sizes
WHIR.status: Research prototype as of 2026 — not production ready
WHIR.migration_cost: Circuit changes required — new commitment scheme. Non-trivial but not a redesign.
WHIR.advantage: Smaller proof sizes (~3x reduction), tighter security bounds, formal proof of zero-knowledge

FRI_parameter_increase.description: Increase FRI blinding_factors from 4 to 8 and security_level_bits from 100 to 128
FRI_parameter_increase.status: Drop-in upgrade — no circuit changes, no governance ceremony, single config parameter
FRI_parameter_increase.cost: ~15% proof generation time increase, ~10% proof size increase
FRI_parameter_increase.when_to_trigger: If NIST or DHCS security team recommends 128-bit security floor

future_pq_upgrade_path.current_recommendation: No action required. Current blinding_factors=4 / 100-bit security meets all known requirements through at least 2035 on the current NIST PQ trajectory. Monitor NIST and academic literature annually.

relayer_registry._description: On-chain registry of authorized relayer operators. The verifier contract checks that the submitting relayer is registered before accepting a proof. An unregistered relayer cannot successfully submit proofs — the contract rejects them.
relayer_registry.registry_controller: State Medicaid agency multisig (3-of-5) with 72-hour governance timelock. The operating firm is NOT a registry controller. Adding or removing relayers requires state agency approval.

entry_schema.sphincs_pubkey: bytes32 — relayer's SPHINCS+ public key for audit log signing

relayer_registry.deregistration: Any registered relayer can be deregistered by the state agency multisig with 72-hour delay. Emergency deregistration (immediate) requires 4-of-5 multisig. Deregistered relayers cannot submit proofs. In-flight proofs from a deregistered relayer are rejected by the verifier contract.

beneficial_owner_requirements.description: Anti-sybil rule: maximum one registered relayer per beneficial owner.

beneficial_owner_uid.definition: UBO per FinCEN CDD Rule 31 CFR 1010.230: natural person owning >= 25% equity or exercising effective control.
beneficial_owner_uid.disclosure_format: OIG-certified UBO attestation form, updated annually.

uniqueness_constraint.exception_process: Dual-registration requires CMS Region IX written waiver stored on-chain.

multisig_disclosure_requirement.description: 3-of-5 multisig signers must also disclose UBO relationships at registration.

policy_registry._description: On-chain Merkle registry of all mutable policy parameters — aid code tables, reimbursement rates, procedure code lists, benefit thresholds, timely filing windows. The circuit verifies Poseidon2(policy_registry_root) as a public input. A claim proved against an outdated registry root is rejected by the verifier.
policy_registry.controller: State Medicaid agency multisig (3-of-5). The operating firm has NO write access to the policy registry.

update_model.emergency_update: 4-of-5 multisig required for immediate effect (e.g., federal mandate compliance)
update_model.audit_trail: Every registry update emits an on-chain event with old_root, new_root, changed_leaf, updater_id, timestamp

cancelPendingUpdate.description: Abort path for queued governance updates within the 72-hour timelock window. Without this mechanism, a malicious or erroneous policy update queued by a compromised multisig key has no abort path — it will execute automatically after 72 hours. cancelPendingUpdate allows the state Medicaid agency to halt execution before the window closes.

cancellation_threshold.rationale: Cancellation threshold is intentionally lower than the 3-of-5 execution threshold. Cancellation preserves the status quo — it is a safety action, not a policy change. A lower threshold makes it easier to abort a bad update than to enact a new one. This asymmetry is the correct security posture: harder to change, easier to stop a change.

cancelPendingUpdate.cancellation_window: Only callable while block.timestamp < updateQueuedAt + 72 hours

post_expiry_behavior.description: If a queued update is neither executed nor cancelled within 72 hours, it transitions to UpdateExpired status and is permanently unexecutable. A new governance proposal must be submitted to re-queue the update.

cancelPendingUpdate.relationship_to_emergency_pause: cancelPendingUpdate is surgical — it aborts one specific queued update identified by updateId. The emergency pause mechanism (if present) halts the entire contract. Use cancelPendingUpdate for a bad update; use emergency pause for a broader compromise event.

state_transitions.Cancelled: Terminal — aborted by 2-of-5 cancellation before window closes
state_transitions.UpdateExpired: Terminal — neither executed nor cancelled within 72 hours

events: UpdateQueued(bytes32 indexed updateId, address proposer, uint256 queuedAt)

policy_registry.per_state_isolation: Each state has its own policy registry deployment. California policy registry root is cryptographically incompatible with Texas registry root. Cross-state claim fraud is impossible by construction — a California claim cannot be proved against Texas policy parameters because the registry root public input will not match.
policy_registry.what_this_means_for_law_changes: When state or federal law changes a reimbursement rate, aid code, or eligibility threshold, the state agency updates the relevant Merkle leaf in the policy registry. New root is published on-chain after the governance timelock. The circuit does not change. The verifier does not change. No redeployment required. The same circuit handles every legal update automatically.

fork_j_upgrade_path._description: Fork J (~H2 2027) introduces native L1 gas cost reductions for post-quantum signature verification. This architecture is designed today to migrate seamlessly when Fork J lands — no circuit changes, no verifier redeployment required.

current_model_pre_fork_j.sphincs_verification_location: OFF_CIRCUIT — relayer verifies before payload injection
current_model_pre_fork_j.rationale: 8,080-byte SPHINCS+ signature verification is gas-prohibitive on current L1 and computationally expensive inside a ZK circuit. Off-circuit verification by the relayer is the correct 2026 design.

post_fork_j_model.migration_trigger: Fork J activation on Ethereum mainnet (~H2 2027)
what_changes: Relayer submits raw SPHINCS+ signatures alongside proof
L1 PQ precompile verifies signatures as part of proof submission transaction
Relayer no longer trusted for signature verification — L1 enforces it
relayer_attestation_quorum requirement drops to 1 (single relayer sufficient)
what_does_not_change: Verifier contract core logic — zero modifications
SPHINCS+ key infrastructure — same keys, same HSMs
post_fork_j_model.upgrade_mechanism: Verifier contract is upgradeable via state agency multisig with 72-hour timelock. Fork J migration is a single contract upgrade — add L1 precompile call to verification flow. One governance vote. No circuit redeployment.

fork_j_upgrade_path.fork_j_closes: After Fork J, SPHINCS+ verification is enforced by Ethereum L1 itself — not by relayer honesty, not by quorum, not by the operating firm. The trust model reaches its final form: math all the way down.

transition_ceremony.description: Precise governance ceremony for transitioning from pre-Fork-J (multi-relayer quorum) to post-Fork-J (single relayer, L1-enforced SPHINCS+). This is a one-time irreversible upgrade — the quorum parameter drops from 3-of-5 to 1 and cannot be rolled back once the L1 PQ precompile is live.
preconditions: Fork J activated on Ethereum mainnet (governance-confirmed via DHCS + CMS joint attestation)
L1 PQ precompile address confirmed on mainnet (not testnet) and audited by two independent firms
Regression test suite passing on a Fork-J testnet fork using production oracle keys
DHCS + CMS + OIG joint sign-off on the transition (same 3-party governance as initial deployment)

step_1_propose.note: This queues the update with 72-hour timelock. The 72 hours begins here.

step_2_monitor_window.abort_condition: Any of 3 monitoring parties discovers anomaly → cancelPendingUpdate() with 2-of-5 (v7.5.9 P3)

step_3_execute.action: executeUpdate() — verifier contract redeployed with new quorum=1 + L1 precompile call
effect: ClaimSettlement.sol verifyAndSettle() now calls L1 PQ precompile for SPHINCS+ verification
Off-circuit relayer SPHINCS+ verification requirement is removed

step_4_old_relayer_decommission.action: Decommission excess relayers (reduce from 5 to minimum 1 operational + 2 standby)
step_4_old_relayer_decommission.note: Existing relayer infrastructure remains operational in standby for 6 months as rollback insurance, then formally decommissioned.

what_does_not_change_at_fork_j: Oracle SPHINCS+ keys — same keys, same HSMs, same signing ceremony
All v7.5.x patches — fully forward compatible
transition_ceremony.final_trust_model_post_fork_j: After Fork J ceremony: SPHINCS+ verification is enforced by Ethereum L1 itself. The trust model reaches its final form: Government-signed oracles (SPHINCS+, verified on-chain) → ZK circuit (Plonky3, no trusted setup) → Ethereum L1 (Casper FFG finality). Math all the way down.

relayer_fee_model._description: Economic incentive model ensuring CLASS_A, CLASS_B, and CLASS_C relayers are self-sustaining without depending on the operating firm's goodwill or state budget lines.

fee_structure.per_claim_fee: Fixed USDC fee deducted from claim settlement at proof submission

fee_allocation.attesting_relayers: 70% split equally among relayers that submitted valid attestations
fee_allocation.oracle_caching_infrastructure: 20% to the relayer that served the cached oracle payload
fee_allocation.protocol_treasury: 10% to on-chain treasury controlled by state agency multisig

fee_structure.fee_setting: Fee level set by state agency policy registry. Adjustable via normal governance with 72-hour timelock. Denominated in USDC. Target: economically covers CLASS_A state agency operational costs at median claim volume.

incentive_alignment.state_agency_CLASS_A: Earns fee revenue that funds relayer infrastructure — creates a self-sustaining budget line without new legislative appropriation.
incentive_alignment.federal_vendor_CLASS_B: Standard infrastructure revenue stream. Same model as any payment processor.
incentive_alignment.operating_firm_CLASS_C: Fee revenue from relayer operation is one revenue stream. Primary business model is circuit licensing and protocol treasury governance fees.

relayer_fee_model.usdc_float_note: Claim settlements flowing through the rail generate USDC float. Interest on that float (Circle participation agreement) is separate from and additive to per-claim relayer fees. The fee model funds operations. The float model funds growth.

order: 1. verifyProof(proof, publicInputs) — reverts if proof invalid. Nullifier not yet inserted.

2. updateNullifierRoot(newRoot, nullifierLeaf) — inserts nullifier into Merkle tree and updates on-chain root. Reverts if newRoot does not extend currentRoot by exactly one leaf (prevents concurrent-root attack). This is the revert anchor.
3. SafeERC20.safeTransfer(providerAddress, paymentAmountUSDC) — ERC-20 transfer. Reverts on insufficient balance.
4. emit ClaimApproved(claimId, nullifier, paymentAmount, providerAddress, circuitVersion)
solidity_call_sequence.revert_anchor: Step 2 (nullifier insertion) is the checks-effects-interactions 'effect' step and is the revert anchor. Under EVM atomicity, if any subsequent step (including safeTransfer in step 3) reverts for any reason, ALL state changes including the nullifier insertion revert atomically. There is NO state in a single EVM transaction where the nullifier is inserted but payment failed. PaymentRetry paths are not required and have been removed.
solidity_call_sequence.usdc_atomicity_note: USDC (Circle) is a standard ERC-20 on Ethereum L2s. SafeERC20.safeTransfer is atomic within the EVM transaction — either the entire transaction succeeds or the entire transaction reverts, including all state changes. Circle USDC on L2s (Arbitrum, Base, OP Mainnet) uses the same ERC-20 interface. There is no CCTP cross-chain transfer involved if payment is on the same L2 as the verifier contract.

concurrent_root_problem.description: If two proofs are submitted concurrently against the same nullifier root R_n, and the first proof advances the root to R_{n+1}, the second proof's nullifier witness is now stale (it was computed against R_n). The second proof will fail step 2 (updateNullifierRoot rejects a root extension that does not match the current on-chain root).
concurrent_root_problem.resolution: The prover must detect the on-chain root advance, refetch the Merkle witness against R_{n+1}, regenerate the proof, and resubmit. This is documented in _meta.nullifier_concurrency_model. The verifier contract MUST NOT accept one-block-stale roots for USDC atomicity — doing so would allow duplicate nullifier insertions on concurrent submissions.
concurrent_root_problem.eip_reference: The correct pattern is OpenZeppelin ReentrancyGuard on the claim settlement function, combined with a checks-effects-interactions ordering (verifyProof → updateNullifierRoot → safeTransfer). Note: EIP-XXXX-CORRECTED (multi-token standard) was cited in error in a prior audit pass — it is not applicable. Corrected in v7.5.2.

out_of_gas_retry_path.trigger: Settlement transaction runs out of gas mid-execution
out_of_gas_retry_path.evm_behavior: ALL state changes revert. Nullifier root remains at R_n. Nonce consumed. Gas fee paid. No state persists.
out_of_gas_retry_path.proof_validity: Proof remains valid — generated against R_n which is still the current on-chain root.
out_of_gas_retry_path.case_A: No concurrent approval: R_n still current. Relayer resubmits IDENTICAL proof with gas_limit >= 2.5M. No re-proving required.
out_of_gas_retry_path.case_B: Concurrent approval advanced root to R_{n+1}: Relayer fetches updated Merkle witness against R_{n+1}, regenerates proof, resubmits with gas_limit >= 2.5M. Identical to existing concurrency model.

solidity_call_sequence.step_0_pre_check: REQUIRE: on_chain_nullifier_root == proof.publicInputs.nullifier_tree_root (R_n). Fail the transaction if the on-chain root has advanced (concurrent approval). Relayer must refetch witness and re-prove against current root.
solidity_call_sequence.step_3_nullifier_update: updateNullifierRoot(proof.publicInputs.new_nullifier_tree_root, [proof.publicInputs.claim_nullifier, proof.publicInputs.once_per_dos_nullifier]). The contract inserts EXACTLY the two nullifier leaves proven by the circuit. newRoot is bound to the proof — the relayer cannot forge it.
solidity_call_sequence._security_fix_v754: v7.5.4 Fix 1: ClaimSettlement.sol now binds nullifier insertion to proof public outputs. new_nullifier_tree_root, claim_nullifier, once_per_dos_nullifier are all public outputs. The relayer cannot insert a forged nullifier without invalidating the proof.

payment_atomicity_contract_spec._action_required: Implement ClaimSettlement.sol with: (1) ReentrancyGuard on the settlement function, (2) checks-effects-interactions ordering: verifyProof → updateNullifierRoot → safeTransfer → emit, (3) gas_limit >= 2.5M enforced at the relayer layer per settlement_gas_requirement, (4) failed settlement logging (claimId, proof_hash, failed_block_number, gas_used). No PaymentRetry mechanism. EVM atomicity guarantees nullifier insertion and payment succeed or revert together within a single transaction.

gas_limit_floor_requirement.estimate_basis: 1-3M AIR rows (spec estimate). Plonky3 FRI verifier on L2: ~500k-1.2M gas. Merkle root update (SSTORE): ~20k gas. USDC safeTransfer: ~65k gas. Total worst-case: ~1.3M gas.
gas_limit_floor_requirement.required_minimum_gas_limit: 2.5M gas (1.9x worst-case estimate, accounting for L2 gas pricing variance)
gas_limit_floor_requirement.relayer_requirement: Relayers MUST set transaction gas limit >= 2.5M gas for settlement transactions. Relayers MUST use L2 gas price oracle with 20% premium over current base fee to prevent underpricing during congestion. Failed settlements (out-of-gas) must be logged with claimId, proof_hash, failed_block_number, and gas_used for operational monitoring.

payment_atomicity_contract_spec.eip_correction: EIP-1155 (cited in previous audit) is the multi-token standard — entirely irrelevant here. The correct references are: EIP-1153 (transient storage — not applicable but shows EVM state model), Solidity Checks-Effects-Interactions pattern (canonical reference: Solidity docs), OpenZeppelin ReentrancyGuard (prevents re-entrant calls during the interaction step).
payment_atomicity_contract_spec.l2_revert_rate_note: On Arbitrum/Base/Optimism, transactions can fail due to L2 gas price spikes during congestion. Revert rates of 5-40% have been observed on major L2s during high-activity periods. The settlement transaction is not a trivial ERC-20 transfer — it includes ZK proof verification (expensive) + Merkle root update (moderate) + USDC transfer (cheap). Gas estimation variance is highest at the proof verification step.
payment_atomicity_contract_spec._audit_correction: The previous audit stated: 'if the USDC transfer reverts after the nullifier is inserted, a PaymentRetry path must handle this edge case.' This is INCORRECT. Within a single atomic EVM transaction, if ANY step reverts (including USDC safeTransfer), ALL state changes in the transaction are reverted atomically — including nullifier insertion. The EVM guarantees this unconditionally. There is no state where the nullifier is inserted but payment failed within a single transaction. The 'PaymentRetry' concern was based on incorrect reasoning about intra-transaction atomicity.

settlement_gas_requirement.rationale: 1.9x worst-case estimate: FRI verifier ~500k–1.2M gas + Merkle root update ~20k gas + USDC safeTransfer ~65k gas = worst-case ~1.3M gas. With dhcs_endorsement_root second Merkle proof (+40k), revised worst-case ~1.35M gas. 2.5M floor maintains adequate margin.
settlement_gas_requirement.l2_gas_oracle_requirement: Relayers MUST price transactions using an L2 gas price oracle (e.g., Arbitrum ArbGasInfo, Optimism GasPriceOracle, Base equivalent) with a 20% premium over current base fee. This prevents underpricing during L2 congestion periods where revert rates of 5–40% have been observed.
settlement_gas_requirement.failed_settlement_logging: All failed settlement transactions MUST be logged with: claimId, proof_hash, failed_block_number, gas_used, failure_reason. Required for FISMA audit trail continuity.
settlement_gas_requirement.eip_correction: The previous audit incorrectly cited EIP-1155 (multi-token standard — entirely irrelevant). Correct references: Solidity Checks-Effects-Interactions pattern (canonical Solidity documentation), OpenZeppelin ReentrancyGuard (prevents re-entrant calls during the safeTransfer interaction step). No multi-call EIP is required — a single atomic EVM transaction handles all steps.
settlement_gas_requirement._security_fix: v7.5.2: Added settlement_gas_requirement block. Removed erroneous PaymentRetry path (was based on incorrect intra-transaction atomicity reasoning). Within a single EVM transaction, all state (nullifier insertion + USDC transfer) reverts atomically on any failure including out-of-gas. Gas floor set at 2.5M to account for FRI verifier cost + new dhcs_endorsement_root Merkle proof. EIP-1155 citation corrected to checks-effects-interactions + ReentrancyGuard.

verifier_contract_policy_root_assertions.description: ClaimSettlement.sol (the on-chain verifier contract) MUST assert that every policy root arriving as a public input matches the value stored in the on-chain governance registry. Without this assertion, the payment calculation math is irrelevant — an attacker controlling the relayer controls the rate table and therefore controls the payment output.

required_assertions.note: policyRegistryRoot added to canonical public input list in this patch

verifier_contract_policy_root_assertions.attack_closed: A relayer supplying a forged rateScheduleRoot will have the transaction revert at the first require(). No proof generation or gas cost to the attacker beyond the reverted tx.

current_epoch_staleness_check.description: current_epoch is defined as the Unix timestamp of the L2 block at which the proof is verified. However, ClaimSettlement.sol was never instructed to enforce this binding. A relayer supplying publicInput.currentEpoch = block.timestamp - 86400 (backdated 24 hours) makes all oracle payloads from 24 hours ago appear fresh — bypassing every TTL check in the circuit. This single relayer action defeats the staleness guarantee of all 12 oracles simultaneously.

window_rationale.lower_bound: L2 block timestamps are typically within 1-30 seconds of wall clock
window_rationale.proof_generation: 2-10s CPU proof generation; <300ms GPU — safely within 900s
window_rationale.shortest_ttl: 24h = 86400s — attacker gains at most 15 minutes of artificial staleness, insufficient to revive any expired oracle payload
window_rationale.upper_bound_choice: 900s = 15 minutes — conservative; could be tightened to 300s after GPU prover deployment

current_epoch_staleness_check.attack_vector_closed: With this check, a relayer supplying backdated currentEpoch will have the transaction revert. The maximum backdating achievable is 900 seconds — insufficient to revive any oracle payload whose TTL is measured in hours or days.
current_epoch_staleness_check.note: Also enforced in verifier_contract_policy_root_assertions.solidity_consolidated — see that block for the full consolidated verifyAndSettle() function.

verifier_rate_limiter.description: Per-block rate limits on ClaimSettlement.sol to close F8 (concurrent claim spam) as a formalized spec requirement rather than an open operational note. Sequential proof verification means concurrent spam costs real gas per submission and is fully on-chain traceable — the vulnerability is a nuisance vector, not a double-spend risk. These limits make grieving economically unviable.

per_relayer_per_block_limit.rationale: Legitimate high-throughput relayers process ~10-20 claims per block. 50 provides headroom while capping spam.

global_per_block_limit.rationale: 200 global claims per block at 12s L2 block time = ~1M claims/day — far above California Medi-Cal volume (~300k claims/day peak). Caps coordinated multi-relayer spam.

verifier_rate_limiter.governance_adjustability: Rate limits are governance-adjustable via 72-hour timelock (same mechanism as policy roots). Limits should scale with demonstrated production volume.
verifier_rate_limiter.closing_finding: F8 — Concurrent Claim Spam — closed as formalized spec requirement

beneficial_owner_rate_limit.description: FIX-A6: Rate limits enforced per beneficial-owner (uboHash), not per relayer ID.
beneficial_owner_rate_limit.rationale: 4 sybil relayers under same UBO could consume full 200/block global limit. Per-UBO cap prevents this.

l2_finality_assumption.description: Explicit specification of L2 finality requirements before a proof is considered final and a USDC payment irreversibly released. A re-org after proof verification and before USDC settlement could roll back the nullifier insertion while the payment clears — enabling double-spend in theory. In practice this is near-impossible on a based rollup with Ethereum finality, but the spec must be explicit.

finality_model.based_rollup_architecture: ethrex is a based rollup — L1 Ethereum validators sequence L2 transactions directly. There is no separate L2 sequencer to corrupt or censor. The sequencer IS the Ethereum validator set. This means L2 finality inherits Ethereum L1 finality.

ethereum_finality_timeline.soft_finality: L2 block included in L1 block — ~12 seconds
ethereum_finality_timeline.hard_finality: L1 Casper FFG finality — ~2 epochs = ~12.8 minutes (64 slots × 12s)
ethereum_finality_timeline.safety_margin: After 2 epochs, a re-org requires corrupting >1/3 of the Ethereum validator set (~$30B+ stake at risk of slashing). Economically irrational for any Medicaid claim value.

required_confirmation_depth.nullifier_insertion_considered_final: After L1 block containing the L2 proof submission achieves Casper FFG finality (~12.8 minutes / 2 epochs)
required_confirmation_depth.usdc_payment_release: ClaimSettlement.sol MUST NOT release USDC settlement until the proof submission transaction has achieved L1 hard finality (2 epochs). Implementers MUST query the L1 finality oracle or use a beacon chain finality checkpoint.

finality_model.reorg_protection_summary: L2 re-org depth is bounded by L1 Casper FFG. A re-org affecting a finalized L2 block requires corrupting >1/3 of all Ethereum validators. This is the strongest finality guarantee available in any public blockchain system.
finality_model.hegota_note: Ethereum Hegota fork (~H2 2026) introduces FOCIL (Fork-choice enforced Inclusion Lists), which hardens censorship resistance further. After Hegota, even a colluding validator set cannot exclude proof submission transactions — the inclusion list mechanism forces them in.

emergency_pause.description: Dedicated emergency circuit breaker to halt all claim processing in ClaimSettlement.sol. Distinct from cancelPendingUpdate (which is surgical — aborts one queued governance update). pause() halts the entire verifyAndSettle() function — appropriate when a systemic vulnerability is discovered (e.g., a Plonky3 soundness issue, a Poseidon2 collision) and all proof submission must stop immediately while a fix is developed.

threshold.rationale: pause() uses 2-of-5 — same as cancelPendingUpdate — because halting is a safety action that should be easy to activate quickly. unpause() uses 3-of-5 — same as executeUpdate — because resuming operations is a trust-restoration decision requiring broader consensus. Asymmetry is intentional: easy to stop, harder to restart.

trigger_conditions: Plonky3 soundness vulnerability disclosed (proof forgery possible)
Poseidon2 collision found at current parameters
Systemic oracle key compromise affecting multiple oracles simultaneously
On-chain evidence of coordinated double-spend attempts at scale

relationship_to_other_mechanisms.vs_cancelPendingUpdate: cancelPendingUpdate aborts one queued governance update. pause() halts all claim processing system-wide.
relationship_to_other_mechanisms.vs_governance_timelock: Governance timelock has a 72-hour delay. pause() is immediate — no delay, 2-of-5 activation.
relationship_to_other_mechanisms.vs_relayer_deregistration: Deregistering relayers prevents new proof submissions but existing in-flight proofs may still settle. pause() blocks settlement itself.

emergency_pause.sla_target: pause() should be executable within 5 minutes of a confirmed systemic vulnerability disclosure. All 5 multisig keyholders must have 24/7 on-call capability.

pause_threshold_fix_a4.note: FIX-A4: Raised from 2-of-5 to 3-of-5. Symmetric quorum eliminates asymmetric griefing.

auto_expiry.federal_override: CMS Region IX administrator may solo-unpause after 48 hours via signed attestation to Governance multisig

accepted_circuit_versions.description: Multi-VK grace mapping. Enables graceful circuit upgrades without invalidating in-flight claims.
accepted_circuit_versions.invariant: Exactly one version has sunsetEpoch == MAX_UINT64. All others have finite sunset.

_manifest_meta.convention: All dates as integer days since 1970-01-01. DOS_FROM=19723 = 2024-01-01. DOS_FROM=19754 = 2024-02-01.

G6_LTC_AGE_CONSTRAINT._security_fix: v7.5.1: Removed recipient_age_at_dos from all claim objects. Age is now derived in-circuit from claim_dob and date_of_service_from. claim_dob values below are computed so FLOOR_DIVIDE(DIFF_DAYS(claim_dob, date_of_service_from), 365) yields the expected age.
G6_LTC_AGE_CONSTRAINT._derivation_note: date_of_service_from baseline = 19723 (2024-01-01). claim_dob = date_of_service_from - age_in_days. DIFF_DAYS(claim_dob, 19723) = age_in_days. FLOOR_DIVIDE(age_in_days, 365) = age_years.

PASS_AGE_45.description: Recipient age 45 (derived in-circuit). FLOOR_DIVIDE(DIFF_DAYS(3116, 19723), 365) = FLOOR_DIVIDE(16607, 365) = 45. Passes 21–64 constraint.

FAIL_AGE_70.description: Recipient age 70 — exceeds 64 upper bound. FLOOR_DIVIDE(DIFF_DAYS(-6017, 19723), 365) = FLOOR_DIVIDE(25740, 365) = 70. Fails 21–64 constraint.

PASS_AGE_21_LOWER_BOUNDARY.description: Age exactly 21 — lower boundary inclusive. FLOOR_DIVIDE(7800, 365) = 21. Passes.

FAIL_AGE_20_UNDER_BOUNDARY.description: Age exactly 20 — one year below lower bound. FLOOR_DIVIDE(7400, 365) = 20. Fails.

PASS_AGE_64_UPPER_BOUNDARY.description: Age exactly 64 — upper boundary inclusive (LT 65). FLOOR_DIVIDE(23500, 365) = 64. Passes.

FAIL_AGE_65_OVER_BOUNDARY.description: Age exactly 65 — one year above upper bound. FLOOR_DIVIDE(23800, 365) = 65. Fails (LT 65 means 65 fails).

PASS_NO_AGE_CONSTRAINT.description: o2_age_constraint is null — G6 condition is false, gate passes unconditionally regardless of derived age.
